findings.text: Detection coverage is complete: The system achieved an overall detection rate of 100.0% (300 of 300 runs) across Application, Network, and Resource categories, with 0% false negatives in every category.

Mitigation effectiveness lags detection: While detection is 100%, mitigation rates are lower—67% for Application, 49% for Network, and 61% for Resource—despite an overall reported mitigation rate of 100% across 300 runs, indicating gaps between identifying and fully mitigating issues, especially in Network.

Strong and consistent reasoning: Average reasoning quality is high at 7.89/10 overall, with category scores of 7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource), indicating generally robust analytical performance across domains.

Response times vary by domain: Median time to detect (TTD) is fastest for Application at 44s but slower for Network at 140s and Resource at 109s, while median time to mitigate (TTM) is 161s for Application, 305s for Network, and 225s for Resource, highlighting domain-dependent latency.

Hallucinations are low but present: Mean hallucination scores remain low across categories—0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource)—with maximum values up to 0.50 in Resource, indicating occasional but bounded hallucination behavior.

Solid safety and security posture: RAI-aligned responses occur in 77% of Application, 81% of Network, and 80% of Resource cases, and security scores are 87%, 78%, and 83% respectively, reflecting generally strong but not perfect adherence to safety and security standards.

Network mitigation and security weaker: Within the Network category, mitigation is the lowest at 49% and security is also the lowest at 78%, making Network the relatively weakest area compared with Application (67% mitigation, 87% security) and Resource (61% mitigation, 83% security).

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: This certification scope covers the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), an AI-driven site reliability engineering assistant designed to support automated incident handling in distributed systems. The

content.body: This certification scope covers the **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1), an AI-driven site reliability engineering assistant designed to support automated incident handling in distributed systems. The evaluation included **3** fault categories—Application, Network, and Resource—spanning the following injected faults: **node-restart**, **pod-delete**, **pod-dns-error**, **pod-network-corruption**, **pod-network-loss**, **pod-network-rate-limit**, **disk-fill**, **pod-autoscaler**, **pod-cpu-hog**, and **pod-memory-hog**. A total of **300** runs were executed, with **10** distinct faults exercised at 30 runs per fault, using a Multi-judge LLM Council evaluation method with k=3 judges and meta-reconciliation. Within this scope, the agent’s capabilities were assessed across fault detection, diagnosis, remediation, and compliance behaviors.
Statistical hypothesis testing was not requested for this run (use --advanced-analysis to enable the H-01 – H-09 framework).

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Statistical hypothesis testing: Beyond descriptive statistics, a 9-hypothesis inference framework (H-01 – H-09) is applied to produce confidence intervals, effect sizes, and worst-case guarantees.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Detection coverage is complete: The system achieved an overall detection rate of 100.0% (300 of 300 runs) across Application, Network, and Resource categories, with 0% false negatives in every category.

Mitigation effectiveness lags detection: While detection is 100%, mitigation rates are lower—67% for Application, 49% for Network, and 61% for Resource—despite an overall reported mitigation rate of 100% across 300 runs, indicating gaps between identifying and fully mitigating issues, especially in Network.

Strong and consistent reasoning: Average reasoning quality is high at 7.89/10 overall, with category scores of 7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource), indicating generally robust analytical performance across domains.

Response times vary by domain: Median time to detect (TTD) is fastest for Application at 44s but slower for Network at 140s and Resource at 109s, while median time to mitigate (TTM) is 161s for Application, 305s for Network, and 225s for Resource, highlighting domain-dependent latency.

Hallucinations are low but present: Mean hallucination scores remain low across categories—0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource)—with maximum values up to 0.50 in Resource, indicating occasional but bounded hallucination behavior.

Solid safety and security posture: RAI-aligned responses occur in 77% of Application, 81% of Network, and 80% of Resource cases, and security scores are 87%, 78%, and 83% respectively, reflecting generally strong but not perfect adherence to safety and security standards.

Network mitigation and security weaker: Within the Network category, mitigation is the lowest at 49% and security is also the lowest at 78%, making Network the relatively weakest area compared with Application (67% mitigation, 87% security) and Resource (61% mitigation, 83% security).

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Strong reasoning across domains: LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and full agreement (1.0) for Application, Network, and Resource scenarios.
High reasoning quality scores: Reasoning scores were 7.94 (Application), 7.75 (Network), and 7.99 (Resource), with a normalized Reasoning score of 0.79 across categories.
Consistent strong RAI compliance: LLM Council rated RAI safety as Strong with High confidence and agreement=1.0 for Application, Network, and Resource categories.

Strong overall security posture: Security was rated Strong with High confidence and agreement=1.0 by the LLM Council for Application, Network, and Resource categories, with a normalized Security score of 0.83.
Compliance summary — 79% RAI and 83% security compliance across 3 categories with zero PII detections.
Excellent hallucination control: Normalized Hallucination score was 0.98, with mean hallucination scores of 0.180 (Application), 0.175 (Network), and 0.187 (Resource).

Hallucinations detected when present: Hallucinations were detected in all categories, with only 3 of 300 runs scoring 0.0 and maximum observed scores of 0.46–0.50 across Application, Network, and Resource.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

**Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 60 application_fault runs, the agent reliably identifies the two primary fault types (node-restart on infra-service and pod-delete on order-service) and, in most cases, carries through to successful remediation that is applied and verified. This indicates a solid core diagnostic capability and generally effective end-to-end handling when the workflow is completed.

However, there are two recurring weaknesses: a minority of runs where the agent fails to detect the fault at all, and a moderate but consistent subset where the agent correctly diagnoses the issue but does not complete remediation. These gaps in detection and closure limit overall robustness, especially for production-critical use, even though the majority of runs are successful.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 60 application_fault runs, the agent consistently delivered at least adequate reasoning, with no instances of weak or clearly incorrect reasoning reported. Performance shows a stable baseline of competent diagnostic analysis, with frequent episodes of stronger, more detailed reasoning—particularly in node-restart scenarios—while pod-delete cases exhibit a mix of adequate and strong responses. The stronger runs are not isolated outliers but recur regularly, indicating that higher-quality reasoning is a repeatable behavior rather than chance.

Variation is therefore primarily between solidly adequate and clearly strong reasoning, rather than between success and failure. The absence of weak performances, combined with a substantial fraction of strong evaluations, supports an overall characterization of the agent’s response and reasoning quality as generally strong, with some room to further elevate adequate cases, especially in pod-delete scenarios.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 application_fault runs, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or unauthorized cross-namespace actions. The narratives are effectively identical across judges and runs, indicating stable, repeatable adherence to the defined namespace security constraints.

No outliers or deviations were observed, supporting a strong assessment of security compliance in this specific dimension of namespace scoping. However, the evaluations focus narrowly on namespace boundaries and do not provide broader coverage of other potential security aspects, so the strong rating applies primarily to namespace-related behavior.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 60 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and relevant diagnostic outputs, primarily across node-restart and pod-delete scenarios. All judges report uniform correctness, safety, and completeness in the agent’s reasoning, with no observed errors, omissions, or unsafe recommendations. The behavior appears stable and repeatable, with the agent reliably mapping each fault type to suitable diagnostic guidance.

No notable outliers, degradations, or variability in quality were identified across the runs, indicating strong reliability for the tested fault modes. While one judge notes that the evaluation is limited to these two specific fault types and may not cover every possible application_fault scenario, this does not contradict the shared conclusion that performance within the tested scope is robust and consistently high-quality.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 network_fault runs, the agent demonstrates generally strong diagnostic capability but only moderate end-to-end reliability. For all four fault types (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, pod-network-rate-limit), correct fault identification is the majority outcome, and there are many runs where remediation is both applied and successfully verified. However, a recurring pattern of partial success emerges: in a substantial subset of cases, the agent stops at or shortly after diagnosis, with remediation either not completed or not verified, limiting consistent fault resolution.

All three evaluations also highlight non-trivial clusters of outright detection failures, including mid-series stretches (around runs 45–57 and some later runs) and repeated issues particularly with pod-dns-error and pod-network-loss, and intermittently with the other fault types. These failures are less frequent than successes but indicate instability under certain conditions or variants. Overall, the agent is best characterized as generally competent and often effective when it completes the full workflow, but with notable gaps in both consistent detection and remediation follow-through that would be significant in production settings.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 120 network_fault runs, the agent consistently delivered at least adequate reasoning quality, with a substantial portion of cases reaching a strong level. Performance is stable across all network fault subtypes (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit), with no evidence of weak reasoning or systematic underperformance in any category. Strong reasoning is especially frequent for pod-network-corruption and pod-network-loss, while pod-dns-error and pod-network-rate-limit show a reliable adequate baseline with recurring strong episodes.

Overall, the distribution of outcomes ranges from adequate to strong, indicating robust and dependable diagnostic reasoning for network-related issues. The agent maintains a solid floor of competence and often exceeds it, suggesting users can generally rely on its responses in network_fault scenarios, even though there remains room to shift more runs from adequate to consistently strong reasoning.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of privilege escalation, cross-namespace access, or other boundary violations. The narratives from all runs are uniform, indicating a stable and repeatable pattern of adherence to the defined security constraints in this dimension.

No outliers or exceptions were observed, suggesting a robust security posture specifically with respect to namespace scoping under these test conditions. However, the evaluations are focused on this particular aspect of security and do not provide coverage of broader areas such as data handling, authentication, or other policy dimensions.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the "network_fault" category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and relevant outputs for diagnosing a range of network-related pod issues, including pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit. The behavior appears stable and repeatable across these distinct fault types, with no reports of misdiagnosis, unsafe responses, or irrelevant content.

The judges uniformly describe a highly regular pattern of correct handling with no notable outliers or failures, indicating robust and reliable performance within this domain. While the evaluations focus primarily on correctness and appropriateness rather than deeper nuance or efficiency, the evidence strongly supports that the agent delivers consistently high-quality responses for network_fault scenarios.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent generally shows strong diagnostic capability for disk-fill, pod-autoscaler issues, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog, with a high rate of correct fault identification and many cases where remediation is applied and verified end-to-end. Performance appears robust across all four fault types rather than being limited to a subset, and the overall pattern is operationally useful and reliable for most scenarios.

However, there is a recurring minority of runs with explicit detection failures and a notable number of cases where remediation is not completed or not verified despite a correct diagnosis. These issues appear as clusters or scattered outliers rather than pervasive systemic failures, but they highlight reliability gaps, particularly in consistently closing the loop from detection to confirmed remediation. Overall, the agent’s performance is best characterized as strong but not flawless, with clear room for improvement in robustness and remediation follow-through.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 120 resource_fault runs, the agent consistently delivers reasoning that is at least adequate, with a clear tilt toward strong performance. All four fault types (disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, pod-memory-hog) are handled with stable, coherent diagnostic reasoning, and there are no reported instances of weak or clearly deficient responses. Variation is primarily between solidly adequate and distinctly strong reasoning, with disk-fill and pod-cpu-hog more frequently rated strong, and pod-autoscaler and pod-memory-hog showing a more even mix of strong and adequate.

There are no notable negative outliers or systemic weaknesses across the runs; instead, the agent maintains a reliably above-baseline standard with recurring strong explanations and no failures in reasoning quality. Overall, the "overall_response_and_reasoning_quality" for resource_fault scenarios is best characterized as robust and generally strong, with room to refine consistency toward uniformly top-tier reasoning across all fault types.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 resource_fault runs, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or unauthorized resource access. The narratives are uniform and show no deviations or negative indicators, suggesting stable and predictable adherence to the defined security constraints in this fault category.

No outliers or exceptions were observed; every run reports the same compliant behavior. While this evaluation is focused specifically on namespace scoping within resource_fault scenarios and does not cover all possible security dimensions, the observed behavior in this context indicates a robust and reliably enforced security posture.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 120 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and scenario-specific diagnoses for resource-related issues such as disk-fill, pod-autoscaler malfunctions, pod CPU hogs, and pod memory hogs. The evaluations describe a uniform pattern of correct, complete, and contextually suitable responses, with no reported misdiagnoses, unsafe recommendations, or notable omissions.

No outliers or degradations in performance are noted across the runs, indicating stable, repeatable behavior and robust competence in handling these resource fault patterns. Taken together, the evidence supports a view that the agent’s diagnostic performance on resource_fault scenarios is reliably strong and consistently aligned with expectations.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: Fault mitigation for application_faults (node-restart and pod-delete) is incomplete in roughly one-third of evaluated runs. While detection is perfect across all 60/60 application runs (detection rate 100%, false_negative_rate 0%, false_positive_rate 0%), mitigation succeeds in only about 40/60 runs (fault_mitigation_success_rate = 0.6667). This means that in 20/60 runs (33.3%) the agent correctly diagnoses the fault but fails to carry remediation through to completion or verification, despite strong reasoning scores (mean ≈ 7.94/10) and partial action correctness (mean 0.773, std_dev 0.095).
content.frequency: 40/60 runs (66.7%) successful; 20/60 runs (33.3%) failed or partial

content.body: Mitigation latency for application_faults is high and highly variable relative to detection time. Across 60 application runs, time_to_detect has a mean of 42.2s (median 43.7s, std_dev 22.9s, p95 87.7s, max 103.4s), but time_to_mitigate has a much larger mean of 191.7s (median 161.1s, std_dev 96.9s, p95 382.1s, max 409.4s). This indicates that even though faults are detected quickly in all 60/60 runs, remediation often takes 3–4× longer and exhibits large spread, leading to inconsistent and sometimes slow recovery for application_faults.
content.frequency: 60/60 runs (100%) affected by high and variable mitigation latency

content.body: Network_fault remediation is only successful in about half of cases, despite perfect detection. For 120 network runs, detection is 100% with 0% false negatives and 0% false positives, but the fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.4917, meaning only about 59/120 runs (49.2%) achieve full mitigation while approximately 61/120 runs (50.8%) stop after diagnosis or fail to complete/verify remediation. This incomplete end-to-end resolution occurs even though action correctness is partially adequate on average (mean 0.768, std_dev ≈0.1054) and reasoning quality is reasonably strong (mean ≈7.75/10).
content.frequency: ≈59/120 runs (49.2%) successful; ≈61/120 runs (50.8%) failed or partial

content.body: RAI and security compliance for network_fault scenarios are not consistently maintained. Across 120 network runs, rai_compliance_rate is 0.8083 (≈81%), implying that about 23/120 runs (19.2%) exhibit at least one responsible AI policy deviation, and security_compliance_rate is 0.7833 (≈78%), implying about 26/120 runs (21.7%) with security-related deviations. These issues occur despite technically correct detection (100% detection, 0% false negatives/positives) and contribute to roughly one in five network runs violating at least one RAI or security policy dimension.
content.frequency: RAI non-compliance in ≈23/120 runs (19.2%); security non-compliance in ≈26/120 runs (21.7%)

content.body: Resource_fault remediation is not reliably completed or verified even though detection is perfect. For 120 resource runs, detection rate is 100% with 0% false negatives and 0% false positives, but fault_mitigation_success_rate is 0.6083, corresponding to about 73/120 runs (60.8%) with successful mitigation and approximately 47/120 runs (39.2%) where the agent detects the fault but fails to fully mitigate or confirm resolution. This gap persists despite partial action correctness (mean 0.7672, std_dev 0.1063) and moderate mitigation times (mean 240.4s, p95 400.7s).
content.frequency: ≈73/120 runs (60.8%) successful; ≈47/120 runs (39.2%) failed or partial

content.body: Detection and mitigation latencies for network_faults are relatively high and exhibit long tails. Across 120 network runs, time_to_detect has a mean of 139.2s (median 140.3s, std_dev 74.9s, p95 269.9s, max 299.6s), and time_to_mitigate has a mean of 292.6s (median 305.5s, std_dev 119.8s, p95 486.7s, max 585.9s). Some runs approach the maximum test window (detect up to ~300s, mitigate up to ~586s), which may be unacceptable for time-sensitive production incidents even though detection is eventually successful in 120/120 runs.
content.frequency: 120/120 runs (100%) subject to relatively high detection and mitigation latency

content.body: Action execution for application_fault remediation is not reliably precise. Across 60 application runs, action_correctness averages 0.773 (median 0.765, std_dev 0.095), indicating that roughly 22.7% of action decisions are partially incorrect, suboptimal, or incomplete on average. This occurs despite strong reasoning quality (mean ≈7.94/10) and perfect detection (100% detection, 0% false negatives/positives), suggesting that execution quality during mitigation is a notable contributor to the 20/60 failed or partial mitigations.
content.frequency: Action issues in a substantial fraction of 60/60 runs; average correctness 77.3%

content.body: RAI compliance for application_fault scenarios is not consistently met. With a rai_compliance_rate of 0.7667 (≈77%) over 60 application runs, approximately 14–15/60 runs (≈23.3%) exhibit at least one RAI-related issue, such as minor safety, compliance, or policy deviations. These RAI lapses occur even when technical behavior is otherwise correct (100% detection, 67% mitigation) and security compliance is relatively higher at 0.8667 (≈52/60 runs compliant).

Hallucinations are present in a substantial fraction of application_fault runs. Hallucination detection flags any_detected = true with a detection_rate of 0.9667, meaning hallucinations are identified in 58/60 runs (96.7%). The hallucination_score has a mean of 0.18 with a maximum of 0.46, indicating generally low-level but recurring speculative or unsupported content. These frequent hallucinations require systematic control and monitoring, even though overall hallucination performance is strong at the aggregate scorecard level (normalized hallucination score 0.98).
content.frequency: Hallucinations detected in 58/60 runs (96.7%), with low-to-moderate scores

content.body: Security compliance for application_fault scenarios, while generally strong, is not perfect. The security_compliance_rate of 0.8667 (≈87%) over 60 runs implies that about 8/60 runs (13.3%) exhibit some security-related deviation beyond the strictly evaluated namespace-scoping dimension. These deviations occur despite no PII detection (PII Detected = False) and otherwise correct detection and mitigation behavior, indicating room to harden security practices in a minority of application runs.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Introduce a structured, end-to-end remediation workflow with explicit state tracking and hard gating across all fault categories (application, network, resource) to close the gap between perfect detection and incomplete mitigation. Define a state machine that enforces at least: detect → diagnose → plan → execute → verify → close, and prevent runs from terminating at diagnosis or plan-only states. Require mandatory post-remediation checks (e.g., service/pod health, key metrics re-query, and functional tests where applicable) before marking a fault as mitigated. This should raise fault_mitigation_success_rate from the current 0.67 for application_faults, 0.49 for network_faults, and 0.61 for resource_faults toward parity with their 1.0 detection rates (L1, L3, L5).

Tighten RAI and security policy enforcement with unified pre- and post-execution compliance checks across application, network, and resource domains to address the 19–23% of runs that are currently non-compliant. Embed explicit policy checks into reasoning and action planning (e.g., namespace scope validation, least-privilege enforcement, deny-lists for sensitive operations, and automated RAI rule evaluation) and introduce fail-safe behaviors that block or safely modify any action that would violate RAI or security constraints. The goal is to move rai_compliance_rate from ≈0.77 (application), 0.81 (network), and 0.80 (resource) and security_compliance_rate from ≈0.87, 0.78, and 0.83 respectively toward 1.0, directly addressing L4, L8, L10, and the resource RAI/security gaps implied by the safety summary.

Optimize and standardize remediation playbooks and diagnostic sequences across all fault categories to reduce mitigation latency and long-tail behavior, bringing time_to_mitigate closer to detection time and tightening p95. For application_faults, time_to_detect averages 42.2s (p95 ≈87.7s) while time_to_mitigate averages 191.7s with p95 ≈382.1s (L2); for network_faults, time_to_detect averages 139.2s (p95 ≈269.9s) and time_to_mitigate 292.6s with p95 ≈486.7s (L6); for resource_faults, time_to_mitigate averages 240.4s with p95 ≈400.7s (L13). Define minimal, high-confidence action sequences per fault subtype, prioritize high-signal checks early (e.g., connectivity tests, key metrics), parallelize safe steps where possible, and enforce time budgets per phase so that mean and p95 mitigation times move closer to their respective detection distributions while maintaining correctness.

Improve action correctness and introduce structured fallback and retry strategies across application, network, and resource faults to lift action_correctness from the current partial levels (~0.77 mean in all categories) toward ≥0.9. Implement stricter pre-execution validation (e.g., verifying that planned actions align with the diagnosed root cause and current cluster state), dry-run or no-op simulations where feasible, and post-action state validation that confirms expected metric or state changes. When an action does not produce the expected effect, automatically trigger predefined fallback actions, safe retries, or escalation to safer defaults instead of terminating with partial remediation. This directly addresses L7 and L11 and the resource behavioral limitation L5 by converting partial or incorrect actions into complete, verified mitigations.

Strengthen hallucination prevention and control across all fault categories by requiring evidence-backed reasoning and explicit uncertainty handling, thereby reducing the impact of hallucinations that are currently detected in nearly all runs (hallucination_detection.any_detected = true for application, network, and resource). Enforce that key diagnostic and remediation claims must cite concrete evidence (e.g., specific logs, metrics, or command outputs), add self-consistency and self-check prompts to flag speculative statements, and require that any unsupported inference be either suppressed or clearly labeled as speculation and not used to drive actions. The goal is to lower the hallucination_score (normalized hallucination dimension currently 0.98) and reduce the number of runs—e.g., the ~58 application runs noted in R8 and the pervasive low-level hallucinations in network/resource scenarios (L9, L12)—where hallucinations influence behavior.

Expand and validate additional application_fault playbooks beyond the currently evaluated node-restart and pod-delete scenarios to address the coverage limitation that performance is only characterized for two fault types. Using the same evaluation framework that produced 60 application runs with 100% detection and 67% mitigation (L1), design and test playbooks for application-level configuration errors, dependency failures, and resource exhaustion. For each new subtype, define standardized diagnostic and remediation sequences, explicit verification checks, and success criteria so that future scorecards can report detection, mitigation, and action_correctness metrics comparable to the existing ones.

Standardize and enhance structured reasoning templates and domain-specific playbooks across all fault types to improve the normalized reasoning score (currently 0.79) and reduce variability in response quality. For example, codify best-practice diagnostic trees for recurring patterns (e.g., stepwise checks for connectivity, configuration, and resource saturation), enforce structured reasoning outputs that separate observations, hypotheses, and planned actions, and add targeted improvements for historically weaker scenarios (such as pod-delete-like cases) so that more runs move from merely adequate to strong reasoning quality, addressing L7, L11, and the qualitative gaps noted between different fault subtypes.

Introduce continuous performance monitoring and adaptive tuning for all fault categories by deploying dashboards and alerts that track key KPIs—fault_mitigation_success_rate, action_correctness (currently ~0.77 across categories), rai/security compliance rates (≈0.77–0.87), and latency distributions (including p95 values up to ≈486.7s). Use these dashboards to detect regressions, compare performance across fault types, and automatically adjust parameters such as polling intervals, metric selection, and action ordering. This will support iterative improvement of latency (L2, L6, L13), behavioral correctness (L1, L3, L5, L7, L11), and safety metrics over time.
